summary: Fix for failed encodings with format context
description_text: Background The FormatContext supports custom encodings by creating encoder registries with those encodings. It pushes the registries to the ValueEncoder prior to encoding an object, and then encodes the object. It relies on the fact that encoding a value removes the registries. Issues If encoding fails, the registries are not removed from the ValueEncoder. Instead, the next time the ValueEncoder is used it removes them. However, in the process of doing so it walks up the parent registry stack and sets all parent values to null. In the following scenario this results in an NPE, since a registry parent is null. 1. Start encoding with encoder E1 2. Push registry R to encoder E1 3. Encode object with encoder E1 4. Encoding fails, encoder E1 still has a reference to registry R 5. Start encoding with encoder E2 6. Push registry R to encoder E2 7. Encode object with encoder E2 8. Encoding succeeds 9. Finish encoding with encoder E2, registry R removed and parent set to null 10. Start encoding with encoder E1 11. Attempts to clear registries, but still has R with now null parent and NPEs Changes A longer term fix would be to not have each registry hold a parent reference, since that is too stateful and brittle. See linked ticket. For now, we will simply not attempt to walk up the tree and set parents to null in ValueTreeEncoder.reset(). The parent will be set properly the next time the encoder is pushed, and we don't use these pushed encoders stand-alone. Flags This is a bug fix for an issue observed only in integration testing of non-production code. The type is "fix" and areas is "code". The breaking flag is false.

Background Nucleus supports multiple services that derive and dispatch data based on events. Issues As part of the matching project, Nucleus needs to support static guard rails. In summary, guard rails are calculated when a session starts, i.e., it status changes to order phase. Guard rails are calculated as a multiple of of the bid-ask spread added or subtracted from the CP+. See MA-36 for full definition. Changes We are adding venue code to the venue entity. This code, e.g., Auctions, Midx, allows us to filter instruments that can trade in Auctions. Note, due to platform limitations, while we create the venue code enum in nucleus, as it is very generic, we must have in the enum at least one value. In turn, we created a "fake" venue named internal in Nucleus. This will be fixed later. A service for snapping the guard rails when a session changes to order phase is created. For all price types the guard rails are calculated as: bid-ask spread equals CP+ ask - CP+ bid. The bid (ask) guard rail is the CP+ bid (CP+ ask) -   multiplier times the CP+ bid (CP+ ask). For price type equals price if the bid ask spread is zero or negative, price type is not calculated. For non price price type, if the bid ask spread is zero or positive guard rails are not calculated. VenueDefinitions In many instances we are pushing into a service some constants, say venue name, which might not be stable. Our to current platform limitation it is an awkward solution which will become elegant as we make progress. We are adding a field to the venue definition named VenueCode. This field is matched with an enum named VenueCode which can contain any arbitrary set of venue. This field is unique field. In the code one of the enumerators venues is AUCTIONS. This allow us to find the venue id by quering the venue service with the enum auctions. What are the issues. The easiest path is to be able to handle enum names in a more friendly way. Currently enum name equal the java convention for enum names. H…

summary: C&C invocation of static method may fail
description_text: Background C&C allows the invocation of a static method with the "call" command. This is implemented with the same code that constructs an object, since the procedure is very similar. Issues The object construction takes a Directory implicit parameter, which is where the object instance will be registered. The "call" command does not, since it does not register anything. However, in ReflectedRegistrar, a ConstructorInvocationContext is used for both cases. In the "call" case, it is incorrect since the "call" is not a constructor invocation. The cached data wrapped in the ConstructorInvocationContext is used, which when the context has not been wrapped or was recently wrapped without a directory, causes an NPE. Changes Update ReflectedRegistrar to use the parent context directly for the "call" command. Update ConstructorInvocationContext to throw an IllegalStateException if used inappropriately instead of an NPE. Flags This is a bug fix so type is "fix". It does not require changes from anyone, so breaking is "false". It has only manifested itself in development, so areas is "code".

Summary The Matching project needs to handle flex order from clients. However, there is a lot of functionality between FIX and UI interfaces short of the flex orders. The Order Manager (OM) workflows handle an order lifecycle once the order has become a firm order, i.e., a FIX order or a flex's child order. Goals Create an OM that can handle the no-touch workflow of an Auctions order. Non-Goals None Success Metrics The Auctions order manager can handle the firm orders originated from client orders, send them to match and generate sell side messages to represent the results of the actions. Motivation We need to implement DMA workflows for Matching system such as Auctions, that reacts to order lifecycle events and send relevant actions for the order and its child orders, if any. This will allow the OrderManager bot to manage the Matching order lifecyle, which in turn will trigger actions on the edge, such as a UI drop or a FIX gateway outbound message. We already implemented DMA workflows for a Matching system generically, and a bot created for the Mid-X. We need to create a similar bot for Auctions. Description The OM workflows handle the full lifecycle of generic firm orders. For example, creating back-to-back child orders for parent orders, forwarding cancel/replace requests of the parents to the children, and propagating accepts and rejects of the children back to the parents. The OM is fully automated, i.e., it does not stage orders, waiting for instructions. The following is a list of tasks required to implement a DMA workflows bot for Auctions given we already have a generic DMA workflows bot: Creates an AuctionsWorkflowOrder and mutator Creates an AuctionsOrderRequestMessageWriter that populate the auctions supplemental from parent to child Creates an auctions-order-manager-bot commands Creates beaker tests for the Auctions DMA workflow manager

summary: Auctions RTS And Integration Server Feed/Drop
description_text: Summary Creating the feed and drop for translating between the core messages and RTS orders via Integration Server (IS). Goals Feed: A single feed application that can listen to messages from IS related to RTS, filter them based on interest, and send core messages corresponding to those messages relevant to Nucleus. Drops: A set of drops, one per topic, that sends Kafka messages in response to RTS lifecycle core messages. Re-usability: Reuse as much as possible the Panorama/ADX schema.  A preference is to enhance the schema without breaking Panorama. This should be carefully considered as Nucleus is not using schema registry and the impact it might have on schema evolution. Non-Goals Solving the drop recovery issue Handling of lists Any logic to handle asynchronous workflows Any workflows Harmonization with the trade reporting feed/drops Success Metrics The success of this epic is determined by connecting the feed and drop to IS and exchanging messages. This can be done with a core endpoint and does not require any workflows to deem this effort a success. Motivation MarketAxess clients are sending RTS orders via FIX to MarketAxess. RTS orders are staged orders, i.e., they are not committed to any particular trading style, i.e. protocol. One can consider these orders as high touch parent orders. The current workflow an handling of RTS orders is as follow. RTS orders are shown simultaneously on multiple MarketAxess UIs, workstations, X-pro v1, Panorama, etc. A client can assign the order to a protocol in any of the UIs, where it starts trading. When a client assigns an order to a protocol, the RTS order disappears from all the UIs and appears as an assigned order in the "protocol UI"s (or elsewhere as appropriate.) In Auctions we are proposing a new approach. Description Nucleus/Auctions system will manage the lifecycle of an RTS order similar to other systems at MarketAxess. However, the communication with the UI and the user experience is going to be different. Once…

Summary Investigate and update gradle and other configurations to build efficiently in the MKTX Jenkins cloud infrastructure. Goals The goal is to build feature, master merge, and nightly jobs as quickly as possible. Feature builds are given the highest priority since they are both the most frequent and have the greatest impact on day to day developer productivity. Non-Goals We do not have specific goal times in we wish to complete various builds. Rather, we seek to utilize available resources as efficiently as possible. Success Metrics All builds moved to the MKTX Jenkins cloud infrastructure. All builds complete as efficiently as possible. It is not necessary to have fully optimized all builds, more complex optimizations may be left for later. Motivation Feature builds take on average of 5 hours to complete, master merge 10 hours, and the nightly build 13 hours. These numbers only increase over time as as add more code and tests. The feature build times mean a developer must typically wait at least one business day after making changes to be able to PR and merge them. Time pressures put the reliability of the system in danger as developers may feel they cannot wait for the tests for minor updates. Description Engage with the MKTX cloud team to set up a feature branch Jenkins pipeline on as large a compute resource and we can obtain. We will need to modify the build process to introduce more parallelism. We will need to experiment to determine further optimizations and best configurations. We must be mindful of the current build infrastructure and not introduce breaking changes.

Summary The Auctions project requires handling client orders that are not fully specified orders, i.e., they might not have a quantity or side, etc. We refer to these orders as flexible or flex orders. Goals We should create a flex order manager for Auctions that handles the full Flex order lifecycle. Non-Goals Handle one-to-many orders. Success Metrics We can successfully implement the Auctions flex order workflows. Motivation The flex order workflow is the entry point for Auctions orders that come in via Auctions UI, Bondlink RTS or other endpoints. It is responsible for managing the flex order lifecycle based on user interactions with the system, such as new order requests accept/reject, replace requests, cancel request and order submission (slicing). As such, it takes a central role in the Auctions system. Description The flex workflows (referred to here as the "bot") is a set of workflows to implement the flex order handling flow. Messages Nucleus already has a set of Matching flex order lifecycle messages. These are general messages that apply to all Matching systems. If needed, we need to add Auctions-specific fields to the messages (similar to MidxSupplemental). Flex Order Nucleus already has FlexOrder and MatchingFlexOrder business objects. We need to create an AuctionsFlexOrder that extends these objects and adds Auctions specific fields. Similar to messages, a workflow order and a flex workflow order are highly correlated. We should use fields as necessary and factor out the differences such that the flex workflow order and the workflow order share as much as possible. Message Group There is no need for a new messages group, Auctions Flex messages will use the existing MatchingFlexOrder group lifecycle. Main Functionality Slicing The Flex workflow should support slicing orders. It must have parent-child consistency checks. Workflow: Slice Workflow Accept-Reject The flex workflow should handle order and replace requests acceptance and rejection based on ri…

Summary The matching system needs to integrate with Bondlink to book the trades and send them out to PTM. The matching system will communicate with Bondlink through integration server over Kafka (Avro) Goals The matching system should be able to send out matched and DNT orders and receive success/failure acknowledgement from Bondlink for the booking process. Non-Goals Success Metrics Once a matching session is done, Bondlink  should have all the matched trades in the trades table in Bondlink database and X-pro should show the true state of the trade from the Bondlink (eg Done Awaiting Hedge, Done APC or Done). Motivation To be able to send out PTM's and show the status of the order in X-Pro. Description Nucleus will have a fill request drop which will send out fill request and cancel accept events(as DNT) through this drop to Integration server which will forward the fill requests to Bondlink. The integration server will keep sending orders to Bondlink as and when they arrive and will booked immediately. Nucleus will receive the order booking ack using a feed which ADX will populate by filtering the events for midx on a new topic from the inquiry events topic on data platform that receives all events from Bondlink. The event is defined  https://marketaxess.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/MRA/pages/4294934578/Match+Order+Update+Data+model . Nucleus will create a feed to read this data and the RASHbot will process the feed and update the order state in Nucleus.

Summary The MarketAxess Auctions system requires a matching engine to calculate and publish indicative match levels and imbalance data, as well as to perform the auction match process and publish the results. Goals The goal is to develop an auctions matching engine bot. The bot receives simplified Auctions requests from the rash bot and publishes Auctions market data or final matches based on them. Non-Goals Any tasks not performed as part of the indicative or final matching process are not part of this proposal. For example, order entry risk checks or the behavior of the rash bot. Success Metrics The epic is complete when the following is done. Algorithms are described and descriptions approved by business management. Business management has validated scenarios via testing tool and approved algorithms. Bot has been developed and has full coverage in integration tests. Motivation A separate matching engine bot allows for simpler operation. The rash bot handles order state management, and the matching engine handles only the matching process. A design goal for any matching engine is simplicity of the engine. It must not include any state management not necessary for the matching itself. We may express that as the following principles for a batch point-in-time auction engine. The engine does not keep any state across match batches. One a batch is complete, it discards all calculations and data, and starts again with the next batch. The engine receives only orders eligible for the match, ideally with only fields necessary for the auction The engine does not receive any order updates (cancel, replace, etc.) The engine does not perform any per-order calculations needed for the match, such as applying fees etc. Instead the outputs of any such calculations are present in fields on the orders sent to the engine. The engine does not perform any per-order calculations needed after the match. Instead its matched responses can be used by receiving applications to do so. The eng…

summary: Auctions Creates Rash bot for Auction matching workflow
description_text: Summary The MarketAxess Auctions system requires an Auctions RASH (Routing And Special Handling) bot that handles order lifecycle events targeted to the Auctions matching engine, and generates match related events to the matching engine accordingly. Goals Create a RASH bot for the Auctions matching engine workflow. Non-Goals The RASH bot handles only orders targeted to the Auctions matching engine, and does not handle other orders events. Success Metrics Auctions RASH bot is implemented Beaker tests are created for rash bot that translates order lifecycle events into matching engine events and vice versa. Motivation RASH bot stays in between the regular order life cycle and the matching engine and translate the core events in between. This prevent the workflow implemented on the order lifecycle to worry about details of the matching engine. The Auctions RASH bot should implement the following logic in general: During the order phase, performs the following actions All new orders are accepted and replaces and cancels are also allowed Every 15 seconds, performs the following actions Sends a match requests start with type=indictive For every live order eligible to trade, send a match request Sends match requests done When the match phase starts, performs the following actions Sends a match requests start type=final For every live order eligible to trade, send a match request For every live order ineligible to trade, sends a cancel accept Sends match requests done During the match phase performs the following actions New orders are rejected, cancels and replaces are also rejected When a match response with positive execQty is received sends a fill request When a match response with zero execQty is received sends a cancel accept When a fill confirmation is received sends a fill When a fill reject is received sends a cancel accept When a fill is received that partially fills the order, sends a cancel accept In any other phase, new orders are rejected, cancels and replaces…

Summary The MarketAxess Auctions system requires canceling orders for terminated sessions, orders for ineligible users, and state market data. It is a general requirement of any system to respond to certain events by canceling or otherwise retracting certain other data. Note that the other data can be any business object with a lifecycle and is not limited to order data. Goals The goal is a Nucleus platform cancel framework that make its relatively easy for business level developers to add new cancel scenarios. This epic will also include the cancel scenarios for the Auctions system. Non-Goals It is a non-goal of this epic to convert any existing cancel bots to the new framework. Success Metrics The epic is complete when the goals are met with a flexible and performant framework. Motivation The existing cancel bot work for Polaris is bespoke, with no specific framework support. As we build more systems, we can see a common pattern to these actions. A framework will save time by not requiring re-implementation and redundant testing of common features, and prevent bugs my moving more complex logic into the framework. Description The cancel bot framework will work with an business object service, which will hold the data to be potentially canceled. The framework will provide the following features. The ability to index the data in a flexible way, ideally through C&C configuration. Multiple indices may be defined on the same data. The ability to define a "cancel trigger", which listens to core events and data objects and indicates the need to cancel certain data from an index. The ability to define a "cancel action", which is invoked to issue the cancel commands from data, an index, and index values. Once the framework is complete, implement the various cancels with it.

Background The Kafka consumer recovery requires a unique "group identifier" for each runtime consumer. This is to avoid the situation where an VM crashes and the Kakfa broker thinks it is still connected, and a backup application connects. If the backup has the group id as the former primary, the broker will consider it as part of the group and assign it only a subset of the partitions. Issues We need a way to generate a unique group id across all applications. It must change when the application is restarted. Ideally it is meaningful, referencing the application in an obvious way, for troubleshooting Changes Add a new implicit parameter RuntimeApplication. It has the following methods. All must be garbage free. Application application(): returns its application DirectBuffer runtimeIdentifier(): returns its runtime identifier (see below) DirectBuffer runtimeName(): returns the fully qualified application name + ":" + the runtime identifier (see below) It should combine Application with a runtime identifier. The runtime identifier need not be unique by itself, but it plus the fully qualified application name must be unique across all applications, past or present, in the system. The identifier is obtained from the command environment. If can be given a DirectBuffer in its builder for this purpose. If not given, it uses pid + createTime(). For beaker in-proc, we will need to provide one that contains the result of an incrementing counter. Remember to update all the places that need to be updated when a new implicit parameter is added. See comment on line 258 of Main.java. Also see line 312 of c_and_c_basics_developer_guide.rst. Flags This is a new feature for developers only that does not affect existing functionality. Type is "feat", areas is "code", and breaking is "false".

Summary The matching system need to integrate with bondlink to book the trades and send out PTM. The matching system will communicate with bondlink through integration server over Kafka (Avro) Goals The matching system should be able to send out matched and DNT orders and receive acknowledgement from bondlink when trades are booked and done fully on the bondlink side. Non-Goals Success Metrics Once a matching session is done, bondlink should have all the matched trades in the trades table in bondlink database and x-pro should show the true state of the trade from the bondlink (eg Done Awaiting Hedge ,Done APC or Done) Motivation To be able to send out PTM's Description Nucleus will have a fill request drop which will send out fill request and cancel accept events(as DNT) through this drop. The integration server will keep batching all matched orders in integration server for a session and when they receive a fill request or dnt with totalFillCountForSession ,they will stop batching and consider the session done.At this point they will send requests to bondlink for booking. Total fill count will only be populated in on the last message which can either be a DNT or a fill request. Nucleus will receive the order booking ack using a feed which ADX will populate by filtering the events for midx on a new topic from the inquiry events topic on data platform that receives all events from bondlink. The event is defined  https://marketaxess.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/MRA/pages/4294934578/Match+Order+Update+Data+model . Nucleus will create a feed to read this data and rash bot will process the feed and update the order state in Nucleus

summary: Handling of Repeating Groups in The Luggage Handler
description_text:
Background Nucleus provides a simple way to handle luggage in messages. The FixMsgLuggageHandler offers simple way to configure and populate maps of values on the commands. Issues The  FixMsgLuggageHandler  was designed for simple FIX messages, i.e., FIX messages without repeating group. In matching, for example, we need to be able to validate fields within a repeating group. It is very tempting to follow the same methodology we use in the FIX to command writer and maybe add a method like a repeating group extractor. However, this is not as simple anymore. Assume we would like to add to the luggagge a required field say 123 in a non-required repeating group that is defined by tag 456. Essentially, if tag 456 is present, than 123 is required and otherwise it is not. However, when 456 is present and 123 is missing it is Changes We should add few new method addLuggageField(
            UnaryFunction<FixFieldsMap> extractor, 
            FixFieldOptionality extractionOptionality
            FixTag fixTag,
            FixFieldOptionality optionality,
            FixFieldModifiability modifiability,
            long maxLength) { The extractor can extract a repeating group and which in turn used for extracting the FIX tag. The extractor can return null and then a validation error occurs if the extractionOptionality is required. Flags This change is a new feature with additional functionality. It does not break existing functionality.

Background JmhUtils.runAllocBenchmark is marked as deprecated. Issues There are several calls to that method, which warn should create warnings at compile time. Additionally, build.gradle specifies that those warnings should result in failures:  options.compilerArgs << '-Werror' The scope of the issue is unclear, but at least IntelliJ 2025.1 compiles fail for this reason. Changes Replace calls to the deprecated method with calls to its other overload. Reenable -Werror for compileJmhJava in build.gradle. Remove the deprecated method from JmhUtils. Flags This is a change to tests, so type is "test", Areas is code, and breaking is false as this only changes the internal methods used in tests for benchmark.

Summary Trading day status message is produced by the bridge. We need to consume it and react to it (send it to the UI, stop the matching sessions in progress if trading day is closed, etc). Goals Create a feed for the Trading Day status message which consumes it from Kafka and publishes it on the core The system should stop accepting requests (sessions and orders) if the trading day is closed. The system should attempt to cancel any live sessions when the trading day feed changes to close. The feed and the impact it has is tested properly. The message expected from the data bridges is agreed upon and the content is clear. We should document in a JIRA ticket our expected translation from the MA messages to our required messages. Non-Goals None Success Metrics The goals described above are achieved. Motivation The Open Trading trading day is controlled by the business and they can open or close it at any time. If the open trading day is closed, the applications that trade on OT should not allow any more trading. If the open trading day is open, these applications should allow trading. Description Feed and drop for Open Trading Day status need to be created A workflow bot that reacts to the message needs to be created

Background Nucleus generates a Nucleus FIX schema to represent FIX specifications Issues The  fix2-version  element must be a valid value from  com.pragmafs.fix.FixVersion . Instead we should simply define an enumerator from the XSD and use that enumerator instead of a free form string. Changes Define enumeration in the  nucleus-fix-schema.xsd  file and replace the usage of FixVersion from the  fix2-version  element. Flags The type of this ticket is refactor since we are replacing the free form string with an enumeration in the XSD. Area is code as this is a refactoring change. This is not a breaking change since the refactor only includes changing how we get a value to an XML attribute.

Summary The Matching system needs to support working with user fees. It needs to fetch/receive fee data for companies (including any discounts), and apply these fees wherever a net level needs to be calculated. Goals Analyze the requirements and see where in MidX fees are applied and how they are calculated Defined fee related messages and external APIs Create a Fee Service for fetching and applying fees Non-Goals None Success Metrics Fee messages and APIs are defined A FeeService for Matching is created Motivation Matching systems apply application specific fees to gross order levels to calculate the net levels. The net levels are mostly used on the drops (for example, to be shown on the UI), but could also be used for internal logic. Fees and discounts are defined outside of the Matching system, so we need to be able to fetch this data from outside and populate a service that can used to retrieve the fees and discounts. We also need to have a utility service that can be used to calculate and return the net levels based on specified parameters (for example, application id, company id, market segment/product, gross level, side, etc). Description We need to do the following. Define fee related messages as well as Fee business object/entity Create a Fee Service that provides a simple way to calculate net levels based on the specified criteria

Background CODE-5007 added KafkaCommandHandler. It receives data from Kafka and passes it along to a FeedCommandInvoker for decoding and command invocation. Issues There is little insight into the processing. One cannot tell if messages are being received, or inspect the message contents if desired. Changes Add a metric to KafkaCommandHandler that counts messages. Display it in the "status" command. Add a similar metric for errors - count of messages that could not be processed. Add a debug option to FeedCommandHandler that logs raw messages if they can be logged, i.e. are JSON or another human-readable encoding Add a debug option to FeedCommandHandler that logs each command invocation, similar to what we do for HTTP. Flags These are new features, and Kafka is not used in production yet. Type is "feat", areas is "code", and breaking is false.

summary: PLAT Business Object Service Support For New Use Cases
description_text:
Summary Enhancing support for non-order and childless business object types. Goals Change the base non-generated code to use business object as necessary. No non-generated code should have  order  in its name of Javadoc. Support for generating business object services that do not have child business object. Any level hirachial level of messages, even within group of messages, Extending message group names should be independent of the group they extend. Non-Goals Hygiene as discussed in Success Metrics We have removed the cross order group from the platform, and its related classes. Cross order is used without any artificial child orders. We unwind the equity order group as a first class citizen message group and instead extend the single instrument. Single instrument message group removed the matching messages. A new group extending the single instrument used for matching is created. Motivation As Nucleus being used in multiple vertical, the usage of the business object service grows as well. However, as it is now, the naming are very confusing because they are tied to orders. The extension of message group is not generic enough causing a bleed between verticals that needs to have other vertical messages as part of their own message group. We need to be able to support childless business objects, be it session information, "flex" orders, etc. All in all, these examples demonstrate multiple use cases that are currently not supported, but should be. Summary Warmup a Polaris inbound FIX order gateway. Goals Create applications to properly warmup an inbound FIX order gateway for Polaris. Non-Goals It is non-goal to warmup the other components in the system, except the implementation should not prevent it in the future. Success Metrics After warmup, Polaris inbound FIX order gateway should be able to immediately ack 5000 orders in 1 sec, or 500 orders in 200ms. (Assuming the other part of the system is already warmed) Motivation Nucleus received complains from clients who send a batch of thousands of orders close to 1 second before a specific order lock time and got rejected. This was because the client's has not send any order before this batch and the inbound gateway was not warmed. The gateway took around 3 seconds to ack the initial 5000 orders, or 700ms for the initial 500 orders. See IL-3176 Description The approach is to use a co-located inbound FIX order gateway to process fake orders similar to the ones the Polaris inbound FIX order gateway is going to receive. This "warmup" inbound FIX order gateway will sequence events to a separate event stream backed by a separate sequencer using a different UDP network. Details tasks are as following. Fix bugs in the system to prevent meson clients of multiple streams to co-exist in the same VM Write commands file to launch a Polaris core (including replayer) with a different stream name. For example "polaris-warmup" Write commands file to launch a Polaris warmup inbound FIX order gateway Write commands file to launch a warmup spinner Write a new app "warmup FIX client" to send FIX flow to the warmup inbound FIX order gateway. The message sent should be configurable Write a new app "warmup bot" to simulate order workflows and generate Ack and Fill for the warmup orders Create guideline for PE to configure the warmup environment and operate the warmup apps

Summary Discover all the external input and outputs that MidX uses with external systems, and create any necessary bridges between Nucleus natural data representation and ingestion and sending out formats. Goals Create a catalog of all the data points that MidX receive from any external system or data source, e.g., databases, Kafka, JMS, UI, etc. These data points should represent the required one as of 4/1, i.e, they should represents the current MidX in 4/1. Create a catalog of all the data points that MidX sends to any external system, e.g., UI, PTM, BondLink, etc. Create a recipe for replicating any data point MidX sends out to the level it can be  replicated . Analyze each of the data points and determine whether the data point will be send to or consumed from the MidX counterparty and Nucleus directly or via a mediator, i.e., a bridge. Create a set of work packages defining all the needs from bridges such that other resources can build the necessary bridges Non-Goals It is not a goal to define the Nucleus work for direct ingress or egress. It is not the goal to build any bridge. It is not a goal to document how MidX create a data item it sends out, but rather than just define it properly. Success Metrics The following are the key success for this epic. After the analysis is done, to complete MidX project we do not discover unaccounted for data points. Motivation To replatform MidX with any sort of parity, we need to have an absolute understanding of not only how MidX is communicating but what is ingesting and sending out. Understanding data flow and content is usually one of the best, if not the best tool for understanding what a system does in the absence of good documentation. This epic attempts to create a significant part of it. A separate epic should be needed to further document the data flow itself. This epic goal is to expose these data items flowing into Midx, comping out of Midx, their sources, their definitions if they are derived, etc. Due to the l…

Background The OptionalDetailedResult interface has two generic parameters - a result type and an additional information type. It is implemented by MutableOptionalDetailedResult, and used in a large number of places. CODE-4952 has introduced an additional extension to include an OrderRejectReason. Issues The additional information type is always DirectBuffer. Including any other additional information is not practicable, since all users of an OptionalDetailedResult expect additional information to be a DirectBuffer. The result is that actual additional information needs to be carried in a separate field in a separate derived type, and those wishing to use it are often required to check for that type and perform a runtime cast. Changes Since the additional information type is always DirectBuffer, we should remove AdditionalInfoT and make it optional DirectBuffer in OptionalDetailedResult. Modify MutableOptionalDetailedResult accordingly. Then, we can remove MutableOptionalDirectBufferResult. Flags The story type is "refactor" and areas is "code" because this change improves the Nuc framework without changing any business logics. This is does not impact operators or external users, so breaking is false.

Summary Building a Java based  SelectorProvider  that uses in process communication. Goals Create an alternative in-proces network implementation for Java's channels that can be used transparently by Java applications. The Nucleus in proc network is removed from the code base. JdkSelectorService is used for both in process and on the air networks. Non-Goals Implement pipes thread safety Network features not used by Nucleus. Success Metrics This epic is closed when the old InProc network is removed without loss of functionality Motivation In order to create efficient integration and acceptance tests, Nucleus developed an implementation of its network layer that mimic socket based communication but inside one process. This is referred to as an InProc network. One can start the Nucleus system with  JdkSelectorService  or  InProcNetworkFacade  but in the first case, bio is used, and in the other applications "think" they are using nio but they use simulated network. This strategy has two drawbacks. The first, the Nucleus code that interacts with Java's NIO code is not used when the InProc network is used. The second is inability to add non-functional Nucleus network code to tests that want to use simulated code. For example, Kafka uses its channels to communicate with a broker. It does so using the system wide selector. The current InProc network cannot be used by Kafka and hence Kafka cannot be easily tested with on unit, integration, and acceptance tests. This epic is about creating a standardized in process network that can be used transparently by every Java application. Description The effort involves creating a new in process simulated network that implement all the Java nio main components. The Java nio main components are: selector, selector provider, sever socket channel, socket channel and datagram channel. SelectorProvider: the selector provider is the factory for channels and for the system wide selector. A new selector provider should provide channels that …

Summary MarketAxess uses Apache Avro for communicating with X-Pro and may use it longer term for e.g. real-time reference data. To support native communication with these systems, we need Avro decoding, encoding, and handshaking. Goals Add garbage-free support for Avro decoding, encoding, and handshaking Support all Avro types Avro schema creation may or may not need to be garbage-free Ability to send and receive Avro data over HTTP Integration with configurable formats and C&C Avro should be just another MIME type that can be selected, albeit with additional requirements (schema) Non-Goals Support for Object Container files. Success Metrics Ability to communicate with X-Pro and/or other MKTX systems that use Avro Motivation X-Pro and potentially real-time reference data longer term communicate via Avro. We can either request JSON alternatives, or support Avro natively. Native Avro benefits from faster time to market after it's available, versus repeated requests to other groups for JSON equivalents. It can also be a benefit in high-volume areas, e.g., drops, where we can lack the ability to replay due to the volume of data (JSON size x rate). Description We need to add support for the following. Avro schema Avro schema builder via code Avro schema creation from JSON (uses the code builder) Avro schema creation from configurable format (uses the code builder) Avro schema utilities: encode schema to JSON, compare schemas, resolve schemas Avro mime-type encoder Avro mime-type decoder Avro over Kafka Avro over HTTP? HTTP Avro handshaking HTTP sending and receiving Avro messages Open implementation questions including the following. How to handle Avro binary types when decoding. Currently we decode exclusively to our "standard form", i.e., strings, map of string to standard form, list of standard from. We then cast from standard form to internal data type. It would be nice to bypass the casting for binary types, which would probably mean expanding our "standard form" de…

Summary The matching project needs to handle Flex order from clients. However, there is a lot of functionality between FIX and UI interfaces short of the Flex orders. The Order Manager (OM) workflows handle an order lifecycle once the order have become firm order, i.e., A FIX order or a Flex's child order. Goals Create an OM that can handle the no-touch workflow of a MidX order. Non-Goals None Success Metrics The order manager can handle the firm orders originated from client orders, send them to match and generate sell side messages to represent the results of the actions. Motivation We need to implement DMA workflows for Matching system such as MidX, that reacts to order lifecycle events and sends relevant actions for the order and its child orders, if any. This will allow the OrderManager bot to manage Matching order lifecyle, which in turn will trigger actions on the edge, such as a UI drop or a FIX gateway outbound message. Description The OM workflows handle the full lifecycle of generic firm orders. For example, accept and reject. The OM is fully automated, i.e., it does not stage orders, waiting for instructions. In addition, it needs to handle bond link trade booking, distributing fill messages, etc. Essentially, the OM is both buy side and sell side workflow, similar to the cross workflow. Main Functionality What follows is the functionality required above and beyond the traditional DMA functionality Fill event The matching engine matches are only provisional matches. The become bone-fide matches when bond link clears the trade. Once BondLink clears the trade, the OM should generate a fill or partial fill and attached the reason generated by the matching engine to the fill. Cancel Accept The matching engine generates an event that indicates that a fill did not occur for an order. The OM is expected to send a cancel accept to these orders along side the reason. Motivation A very common task in the Nucleus system is to send commands to the core. We can divide sending commands into two parts. The first where we would assume that the developer fully understand the core and the various commands and events from infrastructure perspective and developers that writing business level code that are not fully familiar with the core command and events infrastructure. Many developers will work at the business level and the Nucleus system don't want to enforce them to have deep know of command and events. The system would like to have a framework that can be used at the business level abstracting the commands. Few example for such scenarios. A FIX gateway. A FIX gateway receives business level messages, like OrderSingle[D], and a developers needs to create code that send a command that corresponds to the business level FIX message. The developer needs to validate the FIX message, populate a command, maybe validate the populated command and send the command. Originally, this process involved a lot of custom code that do this tasks as a brute force require understand of commands, and repeating checks. For example, it is used for risk checks, it is used for matching prices where matches involved, etc. Most if not all the FI products to be built on top of Nucleus will use the CP+ data. As such, having the CP+ information on the core is critical for success. Description We should build a CP+ feed. The feed translates the data to an entity object using the translation framework. Then using the message writer framework, the data is validated and sent as a command corresponding to the data. Writing should use the message writers and translating to the definition should use the new reference data translation. We should use the Entity generated services. This work should be in Nucleus as it is common for many products. In turn, this means that we should also make the  minimal  necessary reference data available in Nucleus as well. Summary Create a functioning "midx" module. Goals Allow developers to develop MidX product code without co-mingeling with the Nucleus platform. Allow developers to edit generated code definitions without the need to define them on the fly and learn the setup process. Allow consist setup by one person. Allow having a skeleton system before development starts. Non Goal Any non-infrastructure code that is not required to achieve the goals. Motivation MidX is a new product to be build on top of Nucleus. This entails a group of developers creating code in the midx product area to implement the business requirements. Developing a new Nucleus product requires various setup steps, like creating a module, FIX message schema etc. A possible way is to create the necessary setup for say FIX when they are needed. However, the drawback with that is that random developer create a random piece of infrastructure in a non consistent way, say directories etc. It is preferable to create all the required infrastructure for this new MidX once and document the process. Description Pick up a good name for the system that this module will manage. This will include MidX, LiveMarket, Workup, MOC, Current replacements, etc. Create a module in the Nucleus repository. Create a midx inbound FIX message schema on top of Nucleus FIX 5.0Sp3 message schema. Create an empty orders extension files Create an empty order sender extension files Create a base set of Nucleus applications that create the foundation of the product and can be run as a standalone system. Create Beaker setup that include infrastructure applications only for midx Create empty message extension definition Document the process for setting up a module

Summary Reduce the quantity and complexity of the order service framework and increase its flexibility. Goals Developers should have less surface area to learn. Preferably they will need to understand three concepts, order service, mutator, and lifecycle listeners. Creating consistent and informative names. Harmonizing equity, fixed income and preparing to a more extensive yet smooth extension model. Create best practices for working with order services. Non Goals It is not a goal to create services for orders without child orders It is not a goal to enable creating order services unique to extending schemas. Motivation Currently, the order service infrastructure is very complex. With order services, and order views, and root order services and lifecycle listeners and extended ones, and so on as so forth. This make the learning curve for using the system very large, and one will say painful. Add to this the lack of best practices, we end up with some copy and paste when it comes to the order service views. Simplifying the order service infrastructure will allow developers to use it properly with less ceremonial code and short learning curve. Sketch of work This epic is composed from multiple parts that together create the desired goal of simpler and cleaner order service infrastructure. Root order service Root order service is a constraint order service. We will replace it in the code with order service and eliminate this interface and its usage. Order service view The  AbstractOrderServiceView  is always extended mostly to set the generics and the order factory. We have create a base implementation that can be instantiated and use instead of all the extending views in the various products and applications. All the trivial extension will be removed, and only one non-trivial implementation will remain for the drop. Extending message groups Currently, it is impossible to "extend" message group. As such, for example, both single instrument and equity groups are exposed…

summary: PLAT Orchestrator, Active, and Passive Workflows
description_text: Background Creating more specialized workflows. Issues Currently there are no good guidelines for how to decompose a workflow into small pieces each of which is a workflow. For example, the polaris slice workflow handles new orders, waiting for some external events and more. More than anything, the slice workflow sends orders but might never complete. the Polaris accept reject workflow is similar. In one workflow we both wait for some events and take actions and/or sending messages afterwards. Such workflows where there is short term goal "send something to the market" are conflated conflated with long term goal "wait for user input" in the same workflow. This creates complexity, possible errors, and hard to test, and so on. Based on the book trading experience, three types of workflows are emerging. Thinking in terms of these three workflows provide a framework how on to divide a large workflow into small workflows and build these workflow safely. Note, these three workflow types are imposing restriction on current workflows and are not new workflow types. Changes All the examples in this section avoid discussing various edge cases and provide big picture ideas and examples rather than detail design for specific problems. We are going to create three types of workflows: Many workflows can be divided into alternating phases where in one phase a workflow manages the transition of a managed object from phase A to Phase B, an active workflow, followed by a phase where the workflow wait for some event in order to, possibly, change the managed object state to some C, a passive workflow. In summary, a phase of taking active actions changing the state of the managed object and a phase of passively waiting for input that requires a new change in state. The leaf workflow is a workflow that is used to implement the phase where the state of the managed object should be altered. Active Workflows The goals just described and the desire for simplicity leads to the following. An a…

Background We have both commit() and tryCommit(), and difference is that tryCommit() invokes tryFinalize() first to verify all the prerequisites before commit(). Issues tryCommit() is preferred, but we still have many places using commit(). Thus, we should replace commit() and add proper error handling if tryCommit() returns a failure result whenever is possible. Changes In nbm-structure-encoder.vm, we return null for tryCommit(), which should be corrected. Replace commit() with tryCommit() in the following modules: apps, matching, ngbs-apps, and services Handle the tryCommit failure: a) return false/failure writing result when writing a structure/command; b) add logging if log is accessible. Flags The story type is "refactor" and areas is "code" because this change improves the Nuc framework without changing any business logics. This is does not impact operators or external users, so breaking is false.

Summary Create a unified Feed infrastructure. Motivation In Nucleus we have multiple problems where we have access to some data class and we need to write from that class a command. For example, in a FIX order gateway, we read, lex and parse a string FIX message into a  FixMsg  object which is a data class in spirit and we need to write a command corresponding to that FIX message. In the workflows, a workflow populate a sender with some information and message writers translate these data classes to commands. In the UI feed we create a data class from a JSON and eventually write it to a command. The FIX order gateway and workflows use a similar workflow for generating command, and we would like to generate a framework for easily creating such transformations from a data classes to commands. Analysis In the FIX world one subscribes a message handler with FIX engine. This handler is a stateless  BiConsumer  of a FIX message and  MesonMessage  which is invoked by the engine to process a message. A similar concept of  MessageWriter  is used in the workflows. In the FIX world we now using a simple reusable structure for creating such handlers and a similar one in the workflow world. In the most general way, we can think about the following problem. One has access to a data class A, it has access to a  MessonMessage  it needs to attempt writing a command from A and it should return a result object indicating any issue and additional text if an issue occurred. Graphically, A -> f(A, MesonMessage) -> result In the current code, as a result we use  OptionalDetailedResult<SomeCode, DirectBuffer>  where  SomeCode  indicates a success or failure and the nature of the failure if one occurred. DirectBuffer  is additional text that we can use. In the current application we create  f  as follows: A -> | Validation A | -> | Writing A | -> | Validation Command | -> result If at any stage a failure occurred we are short circuit the process and bailing out with a result. While interfac…

Summary Polaris includes order assignment, routing, and risk checks in the OrderValidationBot. The order flow is order/replace request -> validation -> order/replace accept/reject. We will have a similar (but potentially more involved) flow for MKTX systems, and therefore need similar functionality. Goals Develop an order validation framework in Nucleus. Allow line of business developers to easily write and add their own system-specific validations. Validations may output data (such as order assignment or budget checks) or not (such as single-order risk checks) Non-Goals Although it would be nice if the framework could support Polaris, it is a non-goal to promote Polaris risk entities or code to Nucleus at this time. It is a non-goal to change the Polaris OrderValidationBot. Success Metrics A framework that can support the MidX or other initial real-life workflow. description_text: Decoding Background We decode decimal values by invoking the following method on ValueBuilder. boolean onNumber(DirectBuffer number, int fractionalOffset, int exponentOffset); Most implementations that are ok with garbage ignore the offsets and pass "number" to Double.parseDouble. However, the HttpPostParameterDecoder and JsonDecodingUtils save the "number" as text for later processing, at which point it may be passed to the LexicalCaster for conversion to a decimal. There are a few cases here. 1. The cast type is Double or double. In both cases we use Double.parseDouble(input.toString()). This generates garbage in both cases, and doubly so where the cast type is double. 2. The cast type is a fixed point extended type or an percent type. These call ParseUtils.parseAsFixedPoint, which does not generate garbage but does not handle values with an exponent as may be present in JSON: 10e2. Encoding Background We encode fixed point decimal values with FixedPointEncoder, which converts the long fixed point value to a double with NumberUtils.toFloatingPoint, and encodes the double. This may result too much precision in the output. Changes Parsing decimals is very difficult in the general case, but not in the common case where the number is small. Look at doing so for cast type Double/double, falling back to Double.parseDouble where not possible for us to handle. Use the same approach for fixed point and percent Encode fixed point by encoding the part left of the decimal, a dot, then the part right. Do not convert to double.

Summary The FIX engine should be first class citizen on the core. Introduction Currently the FIX engine is not a core native application. What does that mean? We have created an interface  StateSyncService . This service allows the engine to send requests and receive responses from some arbitrary medium. For example, the engine can use /**
     * Publishes the next expected inbound MsgSeqNum[34] from the counterparty.
     *
     * @param message the message in which the admin command is sent in
     */
    void updateNextInboundMsgSeqNum(MesonMessage message); Similarly, the FIX engine can subscribes to "messages" /**
     * Adds a listener to be invoked when an inbound RESEND_REQUEST[2] FIX message is successfully
     * synchronized.
     *
     * @param listener a listener to be invoked when an inbound RESEND_REQUEST[2] FIX message is
     *     successfully synchronized
     * @throws NullPointerException if {@code listener} is null
     */
    void addSyncInboundResendMsgListener(LongLongConsumer listener); Behind the scenes this interface is implemented by sending commands and receiving events. However, we should note that the FIX engine itself is not aware about the core. The  StateSyncService  can be thought about as a command provider and events listener, but with very limited functionality. As a result the FIX engine is independent of the core but with the expense of possible bugs and difficulties. For example, it is sensitive to order of subscription error which is the case at the time of writing this ticket. Since the FIX engine is always used in the context of the CORE we should retire the  StateSyncService  and use commands and events directly. Discussion One of the challenges with the FIX engine in general is ensuring consistency for a user of an engine. Consistency means guaranties that the behavior is predictable with respect to one code. For example, there are no two processors that handles the same message type. This is true from two…

summary: Convert code to use the pool registry
description_text: Convert all remaining DirectBufferPool and ObjectPool creation to go through the PoolRegistry. The PoolRegistry is an implicit parameter that is injected by command and control, and so is available everywhere other such parameters are. DirectBufferPool Builders Use the "dynamicCapacityDirectBufferPoolBuilder" or "fixedCapacityDirectBufferPoolBuilder" to create a DirectBufferPool. The only FixedCapacity pool in use has already been converted, so that builder will not be covered here. ObjectPool Builders Use the "supplierBuilder" or the "defaultFactoryBuilder" to create an ObjectPool. The "defaultFactoryBuilder" should be used when the pool element's default constructor is used to create it. I.e. the existing code contains "Element::new" where "Element" is the element's class. The "supplierBuilder" should be used in all other cases, when the existing code creates elements using a more complex supplier. You will need to specify the element type and pool type when obtaining the builder. For example: poolRegistry.<ElementType, ListObjectPool<ElementType>>supplierBuilder() Properties You will need to supply an "owner" to each builder, which should be the owner of the pool. It is almost always "this". For ObjectPool builders, use the "elementType" to specify the type of the pool element. This should be a Java Class object for plain Java classes and a Nucleus ReifiedClass object for generic concrete types such as "HashSet<String>". Then use "poolType" to specify the type of the pool. This should always be a call to "ListObjectPool.poolType()" or "PointerableObjectPool.poolType()" to obtain the proper generic type. If using a supplier builder, call "supplier(...)" to provide the supplier or factory method. Call "initialSize" to specify the size if it is not zero. Finally call "build()" to obtain the DirectBuffer or Object pool. Branch Remove any "@Branch" annotation or other registration of the pool. It will be registered centrally by the pool registry and any other registra…

We make extensive use of pools in Nucleus to avoid garbage. Address two issues with the pools. Their use is opaque at run time.  Although pools may represent the largest memory use of an application, the system provides little insight at run time into the number of pools, the size of each, their growth, etc. What is does provide is scattered among the individual classes that use the pools. Pools do not share their pooled data.  Pools are often sized inconsistently and do not share their "remaining" objects. This leads to a loss in memory efficiency. Address these two issues with a single central registry of pools. Each class that needs a pool gets it from the registry instead of constructing it itself. The registry uses the element type, pool type, and element supplier as a key to cache and match object pools to return. It also supports DirectBuffer pools in a similar fashion.

We have two related issues with streaming output. Backpressure: Slow consumers should be accommodated for as long as it takes them to catch up, with fixed memory requirements. Catch up: A client that connects intra day should receive all messages from the start of the day, followed by live messages, with fixed memory requirements. The catch up messages in both cases may be every historical message or a consolidated snapshot as dictated by requirements. It is a goal to address these issues for all C&C type streams. It is a non-goal to provide a single solution that includes the Mold stream replayers, although various components and concepts may be shared and it may eventually be possible. The very general approach is as follows. Develop new MessageChannel that (a) has expanding buffer for a single message (b) outputs in raw format without length prefix Replace use of DataSource/DataSink in C&C front ends (shell, HTTP) with MessageChannel Provide access to MessageChannel from ExecutionContext Streaming command code uses a ManagedSimpleStream/SimpleStreamDriver to output to all clients Ability to use StreamingSequencedStore with ManagedSimpleStream

Let's consider an example. A client gateway might receive an order that it needs to route to Pillar. In that case, it needs to be able to send the order in such a way that the Pillar gateway knows that it should take ownership of the order and send it to Pillar. Oppositely, the street side gateway should be able to define that it can reach Pillar and distribute this information and status information. Their could be other apps that might need to be able to declare there capabilities. For example, an app can declare that it can handle orders that failed risk checks. Another example is an app that declares that it can reject a parent order. All these are examples of capabilities and their use cases. In this epic we will introduce basic capability based routing to the system. Capability based routing come in few flavor and in this epic we do not take an opinion which flavor we should use. In this epic we do not take a firm decision about the exact flavor and in turn restore the system capabilities pre-topic conversion step [CODE-2621], yet we keep many options still open and make decisions that could be easily unwound in favor of other flavors. This capabilities are general and not specialized for any specific business domain. The capability id is globally unique across all capabilities. Capabilities are not tied to any application on the core. An example for a capability is "routing orders to Pillar" The second concept of the routing scheme are instances. description_text: Summary Create and use a Core based time source and scheduler Motivation An important aspect of the Nucleus system is to offer a repeatability/deterministic behavior. To do so, we should isolate business level logic from the system based time clocks and scheduler. If a business logic requires a timestamp for operation that needs to be repeatable of the app needs to be able to distribute its state while it recovers, it must use a time source driven by core events. For example, an order drop that distribute a timestamps, should be able to distribute the same timestamp if it needs to recover from the event stream. Similar, if we recover audit trail from the event stream we should be able to also have the same timestamp generated. To achieve this in this epic we will introduce a core based time stamp and also split the scheduler to so a user at the business level logic cannot trigger the scheduler.

Summary A single security, single workflow, DMA only order workflow Description Strategy - Panorama 2.0 - Confluence v2.0 (pragmafs.com) In Phase #1 of Polaris, Nucleus supports single security, DMA, and closing dQuote orders only. This includes full FIX order lifecycle and also cancel from the UI. In addition, injecting fills and provide an out manually are supported as well. This epic describes the implementation of the trading workflow in a order manager. It is also limited to order single and cancels A DMA workflow accepts a parent order and sends one child order corresponding to the parent order for execution, usually referred to as back-to-back. The parent order lifecycle is mirrors the order lifecycle of the child sent to the execution venue. The order life cycle can be described as follow: Case #1 # A parent order is received by the workflow. # The parent order is accepted. # The workflow sends child order mirroring the parent to an execution venue. # The execution venue sends fills. # Execution reports are sent to the client. Case #2 # A parent order is received by the workflow. # The parent order is accepted. # The workflow sends child order mirroring the parent to an execution venue. # The execution venue sends a reject. # The workflow generates an unsolicited cancel to the client. Case #3 # A parent order is received by the workflow. # The workflow cannot handle the order # The parent order is rejected. Case #4 # A parent order is received by the workflow. # The parent order is accepted. # The workflow sends child order mirroring the parent to an execution venue. # A cancel request via FIX or UI is received. # The child order is cancelled # A cancel accept is sent to the client The scope of this effort includes ensuring that all the Polaris tags are received by the workflow and also sent to the outbound gateway as necessary. summary: Ability to publish on topics and targets
description_text: Topic based publishing allows any application to define and publish on any string "topic". Each published command requires a topic and per-topic sequence number. The apps and sequencer track per-topic sequence numbers, and the sequencer drops any command with a topic sequencer number not equal to the next expected topic sequencer number for the command's topic. Such a dropped command is called a "collision" and the sequencer should log an info with pertinent information and escalate to a warning when there are too many collisions over a period of time for a topic, as this indicates inefficient battling applications that should be investigated. A topic sequencer number higher than expected also results in a dropped message, but the sequencer logs a warning with pertinent information since no application should be ahead in this way. The sequencer publishes each event with its topic and topic sequencer number as part of the Nucleus (but not MOLD) header. The first time the sequencer encounters a topic string, it assigns the string a unique numeric id. It publishes the first event on the topic with the topic string and the topic id. Applications receive the message and associate the topic string with the topic id. The sequencer publishes subsequent events on the topic with just the topic id. Each application listens to each event on the core and parses the Nucleus header. It extracts the event's topic and topic sequencer number. If the event contains a topic string, it adds the topic string and topic id association to its local mapping. It updates the topic sequence number for the associated topic for each message it receives. This allows it to publish on any topic. The application publisher requires a topic for each publish request. It supplies the per-topic sequencer number by looking up the last sequencer received in the mapping described above and increments that by one. It sends the command to publish and notes the outstanding command. When it receives an event for…

summary: Conatiner for executing passthrough parent order risk checks
description_text: Consider the percentage of Average Daily Volume (ADV) risk check. In this check, an inbound parent order quantity is compared to a threshold which is certain percentage of the ADV and a decision is made based on whether the order quantity superceede this threshold or not. This check is an example of important category of risk checks. These checks test an incoming parent order stipulation against a threshold that might depdend on some static reference data. In this ticket we will develop the service/container/app for executing such checks. The work will include developing the service for the general type of risk checks discussed above, with the MAX ADV test delivered as a proof for that the service can function effectivly. The work on this container include various important aspects and design decisions. 1. How to store the risk check configuration in a way which is effective for finding the checks that are applicable for specific parent orders. 2. How to execute the risk checks effectivly. For example, for the MAX ADV check, will we query a reference data service for the ADV every check? And if we cache it, how we handle updates to the reference data relevant for the check? 3. Ensuring future extensability for more complex checks, checks that depend on the execution reports and budget based checks. 4. Reciving the checks configuration and update the cache of checks based on new information The work in this contianer does not include any work above and beyond making the decisions. Later ticket will deal with routing these orders based on the results, etc.
